## IB English B Individual Oral (IO): Format and Timing


![When the teacher says ‘keep it brief’ and you accidentally give a TED Talk.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5cfe743117cb8b65270defbd98beddb9ef41657e-2121x1414.jpg)

1. The Individual Oral (IO) is your **internal assessment** and counts for **25% of your final grade** at both SL and HL. 
2. It is designed to test** how well you can communicate, analyze, and engage in real-time discussion in the target language.**
3. **The IO matters because:**
  1. It shows your ability to use the language for **authentic communication**, not just memorization.
  2. It demonstrates your **critical thinking**, linking visual or literary material to broader themes.
  3. It gives you a chance to show **cultural awareness** and make personal connections.

## Standard Level (SL)


1. **Duration:** 12–15 minutes (+ 15 minutes preparation)
2. **Weighting:** 25%

### Preparation (15 minutes)


1. You are shown **two visual stimuli**, each linked to a different course theme.
2. A visual stimulus may be a **photo, poster, illustration, or advertisement**.
  1. You** select one and prepare your presentation.**
3. You can make up to **10 bullet-point notes** on a blank sheet (collected after).
  1. You may **not use dictionaries, notes, or electronic devices. Preparation is supervised.**
  2. **Notes are for reference only;** they cannot be read aloud as a prepared speech.
  3. The **visual stimulus and notes are collected by the teacher after the preparation.**

### Part 1: Presentation (3–4 minutes)


1. **During the presentation, you should:**
2. Briefly describe the visual stimulus.
3. Relate it to the relevant **theme from the course**.
4. Express your own **opinions** about the ideas suggested.
5. Speak **spontaneously** - generic pre-learned speeches will score poorly.
6. If you **exceed 4 minutes, the teacher will politely stop you and move to Part 2.**

### Part 2: Follow-up discussion (4–5 minutes)


1. The teacher asks questions to extend your analysis. These will:
  1. **Clarify or build on your points.**
  2. Ask you to **interpret and evaluate** the stimulus further.
  3. Encourage **comparisons with cultural experiences**.
  4. Allow you to demonstrate understanding of **target culture(s)**.

### Part 3: General discussion (5–6 minutes)


1. Teacher shifts to **at least one additional theme**.
2. Expect **open-ended questions.**
3. Show you can **connect, compare, and converse naturally**.

## Higher Level (HL)


1. **Duration:** 12–15 minutes (+ 20 minutes preparation)
2. **Weighting:** 25%

### Preparation (20 minutes)


1. Teacher provides **two extracts** (up to **~300 words each**), **one from each of the two literary works studied.**
2. **Extracts are printed on plain paper**, labeled with the title and author.
3. You **choose one and prepare a presentation.**
4. You can make up to **10 bullet-point notes** (collected after).
5. **No dictionaries, notes, or devices allowed.** Preparation is supervised.
6. **Good extracts should:**
  1. Be **easily identifiable from the studied works.**
  2. Stimulate **discussion and interpretation.**
  3. Allow** intercultural understanding** and personal interpretation.
  4. Encourage **authentic conversation with the teacher.**
  5. If you **exceed 4 minutes, the teacher will politely stop you and move to Part 2.**

### Part 1: Presentation (3–4 minutes)


1. **Summarize the extract.**
2. Relate it briefly to the **literary work as a whole**.
3. Share your **opinions** on the characters, events, ideas, and themes.
4. **Stay focused on the extract itself:** generic book reviews or pre-rehearsed speeches will not score well.

### Part 2: Follow-up discussion (4–5 minutes)


1. Teacher asks **open-ended questions to deepen your interpretation. **Expect to:
  1. Clarify and extend your **analysis.**
  2. **Evaluate characters, events, and themes** in the extract.
  3. Make **cultural and personal comparisons.**
  4. Engage in **authentic dialogue** about the text.

### Part 3: General discussion (5–6 minutes)


1. The teacher introduces **one or more course themes** as a starting point.
2. You build connections beyond the extract.
3. Expect to explain, compare, and evaluate ideas.
4. The focus is on **interactive skills, fluency, and cultural awareness**.

<callout type="note">* **Key Differences Between SL and HL**
  * **SL**:** Visual stimulus (photo, poster, etc.)** → describe, connect to theme, discuss opinions.
  * **HL**: **Literary extract (up to 300 words) **→ analyze extract, connect to whole work, expand through discussion.
  * **Both require spontaneous, authentic, and thoughtful conversation, **showing not only language skills but also **critical and cultural awareness**.</callout>

<callout type="exam_technique">* The **individual oral has very strict timings and structure.** 
* **Examiners are trained to stop you if you go over time, **so it’s important to practice pacing your presentation and discussion. 
* Remember that the **goal is not to give a memorized speech, but to show spontaneous, natural communication within the set time limits.**</callout>
